title: Apache Log4j2 Remote Code Execution (Log4Shell)
text: Apache Log4j2 (2.0-beta9 through 2.15.0) is vulnerable to remote code execution: attacker-controlled data that gets logged triggers a JNDI lookup which loads and executes code from a remote LDAP/RMI server. Known as Log4Shell. Remediation: upgrade to Log4j 2.17.1 or later; if you cannot upgrade, remove the JndiLookup class from the classpath. Note that 2.15.0 and 2.16.0 had follow-up issues (CVE-2021-45046, CVE-2021-45105).

title: Apache Struts 2 Jakarta Multipart RCE
text: Apache Struts 2 (2.3.5-2.3.31 and 2.5-2.5.10) mishandles a crafted Content-Type header in the Jakarta Multipart parser, allowing Object-Graph Navigation Language (OGNL) injection and remote code execution. This was the flaw behind the 2017 Equifax breach. Remediation: upgrade to Struts 2.3.32 or 2.5.10.1 or later.

title: Spring Framework RCE (Spring4Shell)
text: Spring Framework before 5.3.18 and 5.2.20, running on JDK 9+ and deployed as a WAR on Apache Tomcat, allows remote code execution through data binding that can manipulate the class loader. Known as Spring4Shell. Remediation: upgrade Spring Framework to 5.3.18 or 5.2.20 or later.

title: OpenSSL TLS Heartbeat Information Disclosure (Heartbleed)
text: OpenSSL 1.0.1 before 1.0.1g has a buffer over-read in the TLS/DTLS heartbeat extension (Heartbleed), letting a remote attacker read up to 64 KB of process memory per request and potentially expose private keys, session tokens, and credentials. This is an information-disclosure flaw, not remote code execution. Remediation: upgrade to OpenSSL 1.0.1g or later and rotate any keys or credentials that may have been exposed.

title: GNU Bash Environment Variable Command Injection (Shellshock)
text: GNU Bash through 4.3 (Shellshock) evaluates trailing commands in environment variable values that contain exported shell function definitions, allowing remote attackers to execute arbitrary commands through vectors that set crafted environment variables (for example via CGI). Remediation: apply your operating system's patched Bash packages.

title: Deserialization of Untrusted Data
text: CWE-502: Deserialization of Untrusted Data. The application deserializes untrusted input without sufficiently verifying that the resulting objects are valid, letting an attacker tamper with serialized data to inject unexpected objects or trigger code execution. Mitigation: avoid deserializing data from untrusted sources; if unavoidable, use strict type allowlists, integrity checks such as signing, and prefer simple data formats like JSON over native object serialization.

title: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
text: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor. The product exposes sensitive information such as credentials, configuration, internal paths, or personal data to actors who should not have access. Mitigation: apply least-privilege access controls, keep secrets out of HTTP responses and publicly served files, and review error messages and exposed files for leaked data.

title: Permissive Cross-domain Policy with Untrusted Domains
text: CWE-942: Permissive Cross-domain Policy with Untrusted Domains. An overly permissive cross-origin policy, such as a CORS configuration that reflects an arbitrary Origin or uses a wildcard together with credentials, lets untrusted websites read authenticated responses and steal data. Mitigation: restrict allowed origins to an explicit allowlist, never reflect an arbitrary Origin while allowing credentials, and avoid wildcard policies on authenticated endpoints.

title: Cross-site Scripting (XSS)
text: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation (Cross-site Scripting, XSS). User-controllable input is placed into web output without proper neutralization, letting an attacker run script in victims' browsers to steal sessions or act as the user. Mitigation: apply context-aware output encoding, validate input, and deploy a Content Security Policy.

title: SQL Injection
text: CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command (SQL Injection). Untrusted input is incorporated into SQL queries without proper neutralization, letting an attacker read or modify database data or bypass authentication. Mitigation: use parameterized queries or prepared statements (or a vetted ORM), validate input, and run the database account with least privilege.
